text: I watched him sleep with his mouth slightly open, one arm hanging off the bed. His hair was a mess and he had pillow creases on his cheek. I traced a line from his temple to his jaw with my eyes, not touching, just looking. Something in my chest expanded until it almost hurt. I pulled the blanket up over his shoulder and he sighed without waking.

My daughter held my face in her small hands and looked at me very seriously. She said I had pretty eyes. Then she kissed my nose and went back to her crayons. I sat there on the kitchen floor, legs crossed, surrounded by drawings, and felt something open in me that was wider than the room. I wanted to hold this moment in glass and keep it on a shelf.

I brushed my mother's hair while she sat in the hospital bed. It was thin now, and grey at the temples. I worked gently, pulling through knots she could not feel. She closed her eyes and hummed a song I recognized from childhood. My hands moved slowly and my throat was tight. I would have brushed her hair for a thousand years if I could.

He brought me soup when I was sick. Not canned soup -- homemade, from a recipe he found online. It was oversalted and the noodles were too soft but he stood in the doorway holding the bowl with such earnestness. He had even cut the toast into triangles because I once mentioned I liked it that way. I looked at the soup and then at him and my eyes burned.

I sat with my dog's head in my lap on the porch. She was old now, grey around the muzzle, slow on the stairs. I scratched behind her ears and she leaned into my hand. Her eyes were half-closed. She had been with me for thirteen years. I knew her sighs like a language. I rested my cheek on the top of her head and felt her warmth against my skin.

I watched my grandmother teach my daughter to knit. Their hands moved together, the old fingers guiding the small ones, the yarn looping slowly. My daughter's tongue poked out in concentration. My grandmother's patience was endless. Three generations in one room, connected by a single thread of wool. I leaned against the doorframe and my chest ached in the gentlest way.

She was reading aloud to me from a novel, doing all the voices. She gave the villain a ridiculous accent and the hero a squeaky one. I was not really listening to the words. I was watching the way her eyes crinkled, the way she tucked her hair behind her ear every few pages, the way she laughed at her own performance. I wanted to tell her something enormous but the words were too small.

I held my newborn against my bare chest. He was smaller than a loaf of bread. His fingers curled around my pinky and held on. I could feel his heartbeat, fast and fragile, against my own. The room smelled like clean cotton and milk. I whispered promises into the top of his head, things I intended to keep for the rest of my life.

We danced in the kitchen to no music. I had come home stressed and slammed the door. She walked over and took my hand and put her other hand on my waist and started swaying. I resisted for exactly three seconds. Then I leaned into her and we moved in slow circles between the stove and the fridge. The tension drained out of me like water out of a bathtub.

I wrote my son a letter for his eighteenth birthday. I told him about the day he was born, about the way he used to fall asleep on my shoulder, about every time he made me laugh. I wrote for three pages and could have kept going. I sealed it and left it on his pillow. I knew he might shrug it off or stuff it in a drawer. I wrote it anyway, every word a small act of devotion.

I found the note she left in my lunchbox. Just a small square of paper: thinking of you. Her handwriting was cramped and quick. I held it between my fingers and something warm spread through my chest. I was sitting in a fluorescent break room surrounded by coworkers and none of them knew I was carrying a tiny sun in my pocket.

My best friend called me at three in the morning to tell me about her breakup. I sat up in bed and listened for an hour. She cried and I made soft sounds and said I was there. When she finally ran out of words I stayed on the line, just breathing with her. The silence between us was not empty. It was full of every year we had known each other.

I watched him from across the room at the party. He was telling a story, using his whole body, making people laugh. He caught my eye and winked. Just a quick wink, barely noticeable. But my stomach flipped. We had been together for twelve years and he could still do that. I looked at him across the crowded room and felt like the luckiest person in it.

My grandfather held my hand as we walked through the park. His grip was weak now but he would not let go. He pointed out the same bench where he had proposed to my grandmother sixty years ago. He told the story as if I had never heard it, and I listened as if I had not, because watching his face light up was worth hearing it a hundred times.

I came home late and found her asleep on the couch with all the lights on. She had been waiting for me. There was a plate of food covered in foil on the counter with a sticky note that said saved you some. I stood in the kitchen eating cold pasta at midnight, reading those three words over and over, and the food tasted like everything good in my life.

I helped my father put on his tie before the service. His hands did not work well anymore. I stood in front of him, looping and pulling the way he had taught me when I was ten. He looked at me and his eyes were wet. He said thank you. The word contained more than gratitude. I smoothed his collar and we stood there for a moment, face to face, closer than we had been in years.

I planted a tree in the backyard the day my niece was born. A magnolia, because my sister said it was her favorite flower. It was a small thing, barely two feet tall, with a thin trunk I could snap with one hand. But I dug the hole deep and packed the soil tight and imagined it growing for decades, outlasting all of us, blooming every spring for a girl who would not remember me planting it.

We sat on the roof wrapped in the same blanket, sharing a thermos of hot chocolate. The stars were impossibly bright and she pointed out constellations she was making up. That one is the big spoon. That one is the tiny hat. I laughed and pressed closer and she rested her head on my shoulder. The city hummed below us and I felt anchored in a way I never had before.

I read the same bedtime story for the three hundredth time. My son mouthed the words along with me, eyes half-closed, fingers wrapped around my sleeve. He knew every line by heart. So did I. It did not matter. The story was not the point. The point was his weight against my side, his breathing slowing, his grip loosening as he drifted off. I kept reading quietly into the dark room.

I taught my grandmother to use a tablet so she could video call her sister overseas. She kept tapping too hard and accidentally opening apps. She laughed at her own mistakes and I laughed with her. When the call finally connected and she saw her sister's face, she covered her mouth and tears ran down her cheeks. They spoke rapidly in a language I did not understand. I sat beside her, glowing.

I carried her piggyback through the rain because she was wearing shoes that could not get wet. She screamed and laughed and held on tight, her arms around my neck. The rain soaked through my jacket. We were both drenched by the time we reached the car. She kissed my cheek and said thank you and her eyelashes were wet and dark. I would have walked through a hurricane.

I made my partner's favorite meal after their worst day at work. Roasted chicken, mashed potatoes, the garlic bread they never let themselves have on weekdays. I set the table with cloth napkins and lit a candle. When they walked in and saw it, their face crumpled. Not from sadness. From being seen. They sat down and I poured the wine and neither of us said anything for a while.

I watched my sister hold her baby for the first time. She looked down at the tiny face and I watched something transform behind her eyes. She became a different person in that moment, and the person she became was more herself than I had ever seen. I put my hand on her shoulder and she leaned into it without looking up. The room was very quiet and very full.

He left his scarf at my apartment and I wore it to work the next day. It smelled like him -- cedar and coffee and something I could never name. I caught myself pressing the fabric to my nose at my desk. A coworker asked if it was new and I said no, it was borrowed. But it did not feel borrowed. It felt like a piece of him wrapped around my neck, keeping me warm from the inside.

I drove four hours in the snow to bring my mother her favorite tea because she mentioned on the phone that she was out. She opened the door and saw the box in my hands and shook her head. You did not have to do that, she said. I know, I said. She pulled me inside and the house smelled like cinnamon and I was home in a way no other place could match.
